# Page Title: Biosciences and Bioengineering | IIT Jammu

Department Vision

In line with the institute's vision, the department's goals focus on:

1. Pursuing impactful, world-class research in distinct areas for achieving excellence.
2. Providing state-of-the-art teaching and training aimed at bioengineering-based product realization.
3. Pursuing high-end interdisciplinary research through national and global collaborations.
4. Addressing regional and national challenges offering sustainable solutions.
5. Engaging in community outreach to popularize science to improve human health and environmental wellness.

#### Gaurav Ashok Bhaduri

An enthusiastic and self-motivated researcher and academic. My vision is "Innovation with sustainability for society". Me and my research group look towards energy effective, bio-inspired, biochemical processes for solving current socitial problems. This is done by involving chemical engineering and biological science principles for process development using energy efficient intensification techniques.

#### Jigmi Basumatary

Dr. Jigmi Basumatary has been an Assistant Professor in the Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering at the Indian Institute of Technology Jammu since February 2026. His research focuses on the development and advancement of diverse optical imaging and manipulation tools for studying biological systems.

##### Research Interest

Dr. Jigmi’s research interests include key technologies such as single-molecule localization microscopy (SMLM), light-sheet microscopy, optical tweezers, and photoacoustic/ultrasound imaging. He adopts a multidisciplinary approach that integrates optical engineering with other science and engineering disciplines, along with rigorous qualitative and quantitative analysis. The overarching goal of his research is to develop and extend imaging techniques to address fundamental biological questions.

#### Mithu Baidya

Our research focuses on membrane proteins, particularly G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs). Our primary goal is to unravel the molecular basis of GPCR function and leverage this knowledge to develop improved medicines for the future. Our research aims to enhance our understanding of membrane proteins and their role in cellular communication, paving the way for targeted and effective therapeutic interventions.

Lab site: **https://sites.google.com/view/receptorbiologylaboratory**

##### Research Interest

The Receptor Biology laboratory at IIT Jammu focuses on understanding the molecular mechanisms of G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) in physiological and pathophysiological processes. GPCRs play a vital role as mediators of cellular signaling and are the largest receptor superfamily encoded by the human genome. Recognizing their involvement

##### Academic Interests

**Research Areas**

Area: G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) biology and Cell signaling

Approaches: Biochemical, biophysical and chemogenetic based methods.

**Research Interests**

G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) act as membrane gatekeepers and are the pleiotropic mediators of cellular signaling. GPCRs are the largest receptor superfamily in the human genome, and it plays indispensable roles in almost all the known physiological processes. As a result, almost half of the currently prescribed drugs work by targeting GPCRs. Our lab’s long-term focus is to understand the underlying molecular basis of GPCR functioning in many physiological and pathophysiological processes. We employ multiple orthogonal and combinatorial approaches ranging from biochemical, biophysical, chemogenetic, and pharmacological approaches in our studies. Leveraging this molecular information will enable us to design better and safer GPCR therapeutic interventions.

#### Nitesh Singh Malan

Dr. Nitesh Malan has been an Assistant Professor in the Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering at the Indian Institute of Technology Jammu since April 2026. His research focuses on neurophysiology, computational neuroscience, and biomedical signal processing, with an emphasis on understanding neural mechanisms underlying human behavior and neurological disorders. His work integrates EEG, MEG, and brain–computer interface approaches to develop biomarkers and improve rehabilitation strategies.

##### Research Interest

Computational neuroscience, neurophysiology and neural oscillations, biomedical signal processing (EEG/MEG), and neurological disorders and rehabilitation, Brain computer Interface.

#### Shirsendu Ghosh

Dr. Shirsendu Ghosh did his graduation from Jadavpur University with honors in Chemistry (2009). Then he obtained his master’s degree in Chemistry from IIT Kharagpur (2011). He received his Ph.D. degree from the Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science (IACS), Kolkata in 2016. After a Postdoctoral stint at Weizmann Institute of Science, Israel (2016-2021) he joined as a research associate at Cornell University, USA (2021-2022). After that, he served as an Assistant Professor in the Dept. of Chemistry, GITAM Hyderabad for two years (2022-2024). Then he joined as an Assistant Professor in the Dept. of Chemistry with a joint affiliation in the Department of Bioscience and Bioengineering at IIT Jammu on December 2024. His area of research revolves around the development and use of new optical spectroscopy and microscopy technique

#### Uday Kiran Roopavath

Dr. Uday Kiran Roopavath has interdisciplinary expertise in biomedical engineering, nanotechnology, and biotechnology. He has research interests in advanced biofabrication techniques such as 3D printing, 3D bioprinting and scaffold free fabrication techniques involving spheroids and organoids. Currently his research focuses on developing functional materials for bone regeneration, fabrication of tissue engineered blood vessels and organ on chip devices for disease modeling applications. He has supervised multiple Master’s and currently supervising 3 Ph.D. students, founded startup (Medoja Bio Private Limited) in MedTech, and has active collaborations with premier international institutes. His contributions span from biomaterials research to translational medical devices.

- Assistant Professor
- vivek.raina@iitjammu.ac.in

#### Vivek B Raina

The general theme of our research focuses on answering the fundamental question of how cells deal with DNA damage, particularly DNA double-strand breaks —one of the most critical threats to genomic integrity..

We use a combination of cutting-edge single-molecule DNA curtains technology along with biochemical, genetic, and cell biological approaches to mechanistically understand how DNA repair pathways safeguard genomic stability. A key aspect of our work also explores how these DNA repair mechanisms are finely tuned during gamete formation, ensuring faithful transmission of genetic information across generations.

##### Research Interest

Single molecule DNA curtains, DNA Repair, Meiosis, Genomic stability, Reproductive disorders

### M. Tech in Biosciences and Bioengineering with  Specialization in Medical Devices

The field of biomedical engineering has seen tremendous growth over the past few decades, with advancements in technology leading to the development of new medical devices, diagnostic tools, and therapeutic interventions. At the same time, the realm of tissue engineering and regenerative medicine in healthcare is rapidly expanding, offering new opportunities to improve patient care and outcomes. To address the need for professionals who can bridge the gap between technology and healthcare, the Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, IIT Jammu proposes to offer a two-year postgraduate program viz. Research areas: Our lab at IIT Jammu focuses on understanding the complexities of female reproductive tissues, particularly in the context of gynecological diseases and cancers. This research emphasizes the significance of both coding and noncoding regulatory elements in the genome (REGULOME), which drive cellular heterogeneity and plasticity. By employing advanced Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) techniques and computational models, we aim to map these regulatory elements to uncover mechanisms underlying disease progression and treatment resistance. The ultimate goal is to develop targeted therapies that address tissue heterogeneity and improve women's reproductive health, Key Focus Areas, Reproductive Biology, Genomics & Single Cell Biology, Multi-omics.
